title: Fluorescence Microscopy
text:
Fluorescence Microscopy
Welcome to the online course materials for Microscopie de Fluorescence, part of the M2 Microscopies program at Sorbonne Université / ENS.

title: Fluorescence Microscopy
text:
Course Overview
This course covers the principles and practice of fluorescence microscopy, from basic photophysics to state-of-the-art super-resolution techniques. It is designed for graduate students in chemistry, physics, and biology with a working knowledge of optics and quantum mechanics.

title: Fluorescence Microscopy
text:
Contents
Lectures cover the following topics:

Introduction to optical microscopy and its modern scope
Principles of fluorescence: energy levels, Stokes shift, quantum yield
Fluorophores: molecular dyes, GFP, labeling strategies
The fluorescence microscope in practice: light sources, filters, objectives
Digital imaging: CCD, sCMOS, noise, resolution
Advanced techniques: confocal, TIRF, FRAP, FRET, super-resolution (STORM/PALM), SPIM
RICM: Reflection Interference Contrast Microscopy
Two-photon microscopy

Tutorials provide hands-on problem sets:

Single-molecule fluorescence with mercury lamp illumination (based on Unger et al., 1999)

title: 1  Introduction to Optical Microscopy
text:
1.1 Why Microscopy?
The cell is the fundamental unit of life, and its inner workings operate at spatial scales — nanometers to hundreds of microns — that are entirely inaccessible to the naked eye. Optical microscopy is the primary tool by which we observe living systems in real time, with molecular specificity. It sits at the intersection of physics, chemistry, and biology, and its continued development directly drives biological discovery.

title: 1  Introduction to Optical Microscopy
text:
1.2 A Brief History
The compound microscope was developed in the late 16th century; the first serious scientific instruments were built in the 17th century. Robert Hooke’s microscope (~1670) already had the essential architecture of objective, barrel, and eyepiece:



Hooke’s compound microscope (circa 1670): objective at the bottom, focusing screw, barrel, and eyepiece at the top. Illumination was provided by an oil lamp and water flask condenser.


Early biological microscopy revealed the previously invisible: intestinal villi, diatoms, microorganisms. The 20th century brought electronic detectors, laser sources, and computational image analysis, transforming microscopy from a qualitative observation tool into a quantitative measurement instrument.



Light microscopy reveals tissue architecture at the cellular level. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stained section of intestinal mucosa showing villi (1) and crypts (2) — a classic histological preparation illustrating the scale of cellular organization.





A living diatom (Pinnularia sp.) imaged by brightfield transmitted-light microscopy, showing the intricate silica frustule structure resolvable with a modern objective.

title: 1  Introduction to Optical Microscopy
text:
1.3 The Modern Microscopy System
Modern videomicroscopy integrates a research microscope with motorized stages, scientific cameras, illumination controllers, and image acquisition/analysis software into a single computer-controlled system:



A complete videomicroscopy workstation (Leica): inverted microscope, CCD camera, illumination controller, acquisition computer, and dual monitors for image display and analysis. Motorization enables automated time-lapse, multiposition, and \(z\)-stack experiments.

title: 1  Introduction to Optical Microscopy
text:
1.4 The Optical Path
The compound microscope is a two-lens system: the objective forms a real magnified intermediate image; the eyepiece (or tube lens + camera) re-images this onto the detector. Understanding the conjugate planes — specimen plane, objective rear focal plane, eyepiece, camera — is essential for correct Köhler illumination and optimal image quality.



Ray diagram of the compound microscope: the objective maps the specimen to a real inverted intermediate image; the eyepiece re-images this to infinity (or to the retina/camera). The conjugate image planes and illumination planes must be kept distinct for Köhler illumination.





Complete optical layout of a modern research microscope showing the two sets of conjugate planes: image-forming planes (camera image plane, specimen plane, eyepiece fixed diaphragm, retina) and aperture/illuminating planes (lamp filament, condenser aperture diaphragm, objective rear focal plane, exit pupil).

title: 1  Introduction to Optical Microscopy
text:
1.5 Contrast Modes
Most biological specimens are essentially transparent — they produce negligible amplitude contrast in simple brightfield transmission. Several modes address this:
Brightfield is the baseline — amplitude contrast only, requiring staining for most specimens.
Phase contrast (Zernike, Nobel Prize 1953) converts phase shifts introduced by the specimen’s refractive index into intensity contrast, enabling imaging of living, unstained cells.
DIC (Differential Interference Contrast) uses a Nomarski prism to produce a pseudo-3D relief appearance sensitive to optical path length gradients.
Fluorescence uses specific labeling to achieve molecular contrast — the dominant mode in modern cell biology, and the subject of this course.



Living HeLa cells imaged in brightfield: the cells are nearly transparent and produce very low intrinsic contrast, illustrating the need for either staining, phase contrast, or fluorescence labeling for quantitative biological imaging.

title: 1  Introduction to Optical Microscopy
text:
1.6 Frontiers: Miniaturization and Field Microscopy
The Foldscope (Prakash lab, Stanford) illustrates how the core optical principles of microscopy can be implemented at minimal cost (~$0.50) using printed paper optics and a single polymer microlens:



Foldscope bill of materials and optical layout: printed polymer micro-optics, paper apertures, condenser lens, and LED illumination — total cost ~50 cents. The device is assembled by folding the printed sheet, with paper flexures providing \(z\)-focus and \(xy\)-panning.





Foldscope images: (A) brightfield image of a microstructured surface (bee eye); (B) fluorescence image showing individual fluorescent beads (scale bar 5 µm). The optical performance is surprisingly competitive for a disposable device.


Lensless on-chip microscopy eliminates the objective entirely, recording holographic diffraction patterns directly on a CMOS sensor for computational reconstruction — enabling very large fields of view at low cost:



On-chip lensless microscopy: (a) a microfluidic PDMS chip placed directly on a CMOS sensor illuminated from above; (b) the chip-sensor assembly compared to a coin (5 mm scale bar); (c) SEM image of the PDMS microchannel network (1 mm scale bar). Holographic reconstruction yields wide-field images without any lens.

title: 1  Introduction to Optical Microscopy
text:
1.7 The Super-Resolution Revolution
The period 2006–2014 saw a dramatic expansion of what optical microscopy can resolve, through approaches that exploit fluorophore photophysics to circumvent the diffraction limit:



Historical timeline of microscopy milestones: from 16th century light microscopy through electron microscopy (1933), fluorescence microscopy (1990s), to today’s super-resolution methods. Each advance opened new scales of biological organization.


STED (Stimulated Emission Depletion, Hell) uses a depletion laser to shrink the effective PSF. PALM/STORM (Betzig, Moerner) use stochastic single-molecule localization. Both achieve lateral resolutions of 20–60 nm:



STED vs. confocal comparison: the same fluorescent bead imaged by confocal (261 nm FWHM, blue) and STED (62 nm FWHM, red). STED achieves a >4× improvement in lateral resolution using the same fluorophore and objective.


The 2014 Nobel Prize in Chemistry was awarded to Eric Betzig, Stefan W. Hell, and William E. Moerner for the development of super-resolved fluorescence microscopy:



2014 Nobel Prize in Chemistry: Eric Betzig (PALM/single-molecule localization), Stefan W. Hell (STED/RESOLFT), and William E. Moerner (single-molecule detection and optical control of fluorescence).








NoteCourse overview



This course covers the full chain from photons to biological insight: fluorescence principles and fluorophore photophysics → labeling strategies → the microscope optical path → digital detection → advanced techniques (confocal, TIRF, super-resolution, FRAP, FRET, RICM) → two-photon microscopy. The unifying theme throughout is understanding what each measurement actually measures, and what its fundamental limits are.

title: 2  Principles of Fluorescence
text:
2.1 Electronic Energy Levels
The optical properties of fluorescent molecules are rooted in their electronic structure. In polyatomic molecules, each electronic state (\(S_0\), \(S_1\), \(S_2\), …) is described by a potential energy surface as a function of nuclear coordinates. The ground state \(S_0\) and first excited singlet \(S_1\) each support a ladder of vibrational sub-levels.
The Franck-Condon principle governs which vibrational transitions are favored upon absorption: electronic transitions are fast (\(\sim 10^{-15}\) s) compared to nuclear motion, so the nuclear geometry is frozen during the transition. The most probable transitions are those with maximum overlap between the vibrational wavefunctions of the initial and final states.

title: 2  Principles of Fluorescence
text:
Potential energy curves for the ground (\(X^1\Sigma_g^+\)) and excited (\(B'\Sigma_u^+\)) states of a diatomic molecule, illustrating the vibrational quantization within each electronic manifold.






Franck-Condon diagram showing the vibrational structure of \(S_0\) and \(S_1\) states, with absorption (blue) and emission (red) transitions. The displacement between the two potential minima leads to a Stokes shift.

title: 2  Principles of Fluorescence
text:
2.2 The First Fluorophore: Quinine
Quinine, present in tonic water, is a classic example of a naturally fluorescent molecule. Under UV illumination it emits strongly in the blue, making it one of the first fluorescent substances to be systematically studied.



Tonic water (Canada Dry) under white light (left) and UV illumination (right), demonstrating the blue fluorescence of quinine.

title: 2  Principles of Fluorescence
text:
2.3 The Fluorescence Phenomenon
The full photophysical picture of an organic fluorophore involves several competing pathways from the excited state:



Jablonski diagram showing the main photophysical pathways: absorption to \(S_2\)/\(S_1\), rapid internal conversion to \(S_1\) (\(10^{-12}\) s), fluorescence emission (\(h\nu_F\)), intersystem crossing to \(T_1\), and phosphorescence (\(h\nu_P\)).


The key processes and their characteristic timescales are:

Absorption: \(\sim 10^{-15}\) s
Internal conversion (\(S_2 \to S_1\), vibrational relaxation): \(\sim 10^{-12}\) s

Fluorescence (\(S_1 \to S_0\)): \(\sim 10^{-8}\) s
Intersystem crossing (\(S_1 \to T_1\)): \(\sim 10^{-8}\) s
Phosphorescence (\(T_1 \to S_0\)): \(10^{-4}\) to \(10^{-2}\) s

title: 2  Principles of Fluorescence
text:
2.4 Molecular Spectra and Energy Quantization
The vibrational structure of both \(S_0\) and \(S_1\) determines the shape of the absorption and emission spectra. Each vibronic transition (\(0\to n\) in absorption, \(n\to 0\) in emission) contributes a band to the spectrum.



Jablonski diagram with explicit vibrational sub-levels and characteristic timescales for each transition pathway.

title: 2  Principles of Fluorescence
text:
2.5 A Case Study: Anthracene
Anthracene is a canonical example because its vibrational structure is clearly resolved in both absorption and emission spectra at room temperature. The mirror symmetry between the two spectra reflects the similarity of the \(S_0\) and \(S_1\) potential energy surfaces.



Absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of anthracene showing the vibronic progression (0-0, 0-1, 0-2 bands), with the corresponding Jablonski diagram. The mirror symmetry between absorption and emission is characteristic of a rigid aromatic fluorophore.

title: 2  Principles of Fluorescence
text:
2.6 Stokes Shift
A fundamental consequence of vibrational relaxation within \(S_1\) before emission is that the fluorescence spectrum is always red-shifted relative to the absorption spectrum. This is the Stokes shift.
\[\Delta\nu_{\text{Stokes}} = \tilde{\nu}_{\text{abs,max}} - \tilde{\nu}_{\text{em,max}}\]



Absorption (purple) and emission (green) spectra illustrating the Stokes shift — the spectral gap between the excitation and emission maxima.


The Stokes shift is critical for fluorescence microscopy: it allows the excitation light to be efficiently separated from the fluorescence signal using optical filters, since they occur at different wavelengths.

title: 2  Principles of Fluorescence
text:
2.7 Two-Level System Description
The kinetics of \(S_1\) depopulation can be modeled as a pseudo-first-order process, treating the excited state as species \(A\) and the ground state (or non-fluorescent products) as species \(B\):
\[A \underset{k_-}{\overset{k_+}{\rightleftharpoons}} B\]
The excited-state population decays exponentially:
\[x(t) = x(0)\, e^{-(k_+ + k_-)t}\]
This is exactly first-order relaxation kinetics. The fluorescence lifetime \(\tau\) is defined as:
\[\tau = \frac{1}{k_r + k_{nr}}\]
where \(k_r\) is the radiative rate constant and \(k_{nr}\) is the sum of all non-radiative rates.



First-order relaxation kinetics of the two-level model. The displacement from equilibrium decays as a single exponential, with rate \(k_+ + k_-\).


The simplified two-level Jablonski diagram makes explicit the competition between radiative (\(\Gamma = k_r\)) and non-radiative (\(k_{nr}\)) decay pathways from \(S_1\):



Simplified two-level Jablonski diagram: absorption (\(h\nu_A\)) promotes the molecule to the upper vibrational levels of \(S_1\); after rapid relaxation (\(10^{-12}\) s) the molecule decays either by emitting fluorescence (\(h\nu_F\), rate \(\Gamma\)) or via non-radiative pathways (\(k_{nr}\)).

title: 2  Principles of Fluorescence
text:
2.8 Fluorophore Efficiency: Brightness
The quantum yield \(\phi\) quantifies the fraction of absorbed photons that are re-emitted as fluorescence:
\[\phi = \frac{k_r}{k_r + k_{nr}} = \frac{\Gamma}{\Gamma + k_{nr}}\]
The brightness \(B\) of a fluorophore combines the quantum yield with the absorption cross-section \(\sigma\) (or molar extinction coefficient \(\varepsilon\)):
\[B = \varepsilon \cdot \phi\]
A high brightness requires both efficient light absorption (\(\varepsilon\)) and efficient emission (\(\phi\)). Rhodamine B (shown below), for example, has \(\varepsilon \approx 10^5\,\mathrm{M^{-1}cm^{-1}}\) and \(\phi \approx 0.7\) in ethanol, making it one of the brightest common fluorophores.



Structural formula of Rhodamine B, a xanthene dye widely used as a reference fluorophore for its high brightness.








NoteKey relationships



\[\tau = \frac{1}{k_r + k_{nr}} \qquad \phi = k_r \cdot \tau \qquad B = \varepsilon \cdot \phi\]
These three parameters fully characterize the photophysical performance of a fluorophore. Note that a long lifetime does not necessarily imply high quantum yield — what matters is the ratio \(k_r / (k_r + k_{nr})\).








TipResources for this chapter



Textbooks

Lakowicz, J. Principles of Fluorescence Spectroscopy, 3rd ed. Springer (2006) — Chapters 1–2: Jablonski diagrams, lifetimes, quantum yields. The definitive reference.
Valeur & Berberan-Santos. Molecular Fluorescence: Principles and Applications, 2nd ed. Wiley-VCH (2012) — excellent companion with more photophysical depth.

Key papers

Kasha, M. Characterization of electronic transitions in complex molecules. Discuss. Faraday Soc. (1950). DOI — original statement of Kasha’s rule (internal conversion to S₁ before emission).
Strickler & Berg. Relationship between absorption intensity and fluorescence lifetime of molecules. J. Chem. Phys. (1962). DOI — quantitative relationship between ε, φ, and τ.

Online

FPbase Spectra Viewer — interactive visualization of absorption/emission spectra for hundreds of fluorophores; overlay multiple dyes.
Thermo Fisher Fluorescence SpectraViewer — filter and laser line overlay tool, essential for designing multicolor experiments.
MicroscopyU — Fluorescence Fundamentals — clear illustrated guide to Jablonski diagrams and the Stokes shift.

Problem set

See Tutorial: Single-molecule fluorescence — exercises on photon budgets, quantum yield, and signal-to-noise directly build on this chapter.

title: 3  Fluorophores and Labeling Strategies
text:
3.1 Common Molecular Fluorophores
The choice of fluorophore determines the spectral window, brightness, photostability, and compatibility with other labels in a multicolor experiment. The figure below maps a large panel of commonly used fluorophores according to their emission wavelength and brightness (\(\varepsilon \cdot \phi\)), from UV-excitable probes (DAPI, Hoechst) through the visible range (fluorescein/FITC, rhodamines, BODIPY, cyanines) to near-infrared dyes (Cy5, Cy7, IRDye 700DX).
Three workhorse fluorophores span most routine applications: DAPI (UV, nucleus staining), FITC/fluorescein (green, amine-reactive), and Texas Red (red, amine-reactive). Their absorption and emission spectra illustrate the spectral separation needed for multicolor imaging.

title: 3  Fluorophores and Labeling Strategies
text:
Brightness (\(\varepsilon \times \phi\), M\(^{-1}\)cm\(^{-1}\)) vs. emission wavelength for a broad panel of fluorophores, from intrinsically fluorescent amino acids (phenylalanine, tyrosine, tryptophan) to high-performance synthetic dyes. Structures are shown for each compound.






Absorption (left) and emission (right) spectra of three commonly used fluorophores — DAPI, FITC, and Texas Red — illustrating the spectral coverage from UV to red and the large Stokes shifts involved.

title: 3  Fluorophores and Labeling Strategies
text:
3.2 Physicochemical Environment: pH Effects
Many fluorophores are sensitive to their local physicochemical environment. Fluorescein is a canonical example: its fluorescence intensity is strongly pH-dependent because protonation of the phenolic hydroxyl disrupts the conjugated system responsible for absorption and emission.



Absorption (A) and fluorescence emission (B, \(\lambda_{ex} = 488\) nm) spectra of fluorescein as a function of pH (5.0 to 9.0). Both the extinction coefficient and quantum yield decrease sharply below pH 7.


This sensitivity is exploited in biosensors and to monitor intracellular pH, but is a confound in quantitative imaging if the local pH is not controlled or known.

title: 3  Fluorophores and Labeling Strategies
text:
3.3 A Lipophilic Fluorophore: Nile Red
Nile Red is a solvatochromic fluorophore — its emission wavelength shifts strongly with the polarity of its local environment. It is essentially non-fluorescent in water but highly fluorescent in hydrophobic environments (lipid droplets, cell membranes), making it a selective membrane and lipid probe without the need for covalent conjugation.



Series of Nile Red solutions in solvents of decreasing polarity (top: visible color; bottom: fluorescence under UV), illustrating the dramatic solvatochromism — the emission shifts from red-orange in polar solvents to yellow-green in apolar environments.

title: 3  Fluorophores and Labeling Strategies
text:
3.4 Collisional Quenching: Chloride Ions
Fluorescence can be quenched by dynamic (collisional) energy transfer to a quencher molecule present in solution. Cl\(^-\) ions are efficient quenchers of certain fluorophores (e.g., lucigenin, SPQ) via charge-transfer interactions. The Stern-Volmer equation describes this:
\[\frac{F_0}{F} = 1 + k_q \tau_0 [Q] = 1 + K_{SV}[Q]\]
where \(K_{SV}\) is the Stern-Volmer constant, \(k_q\) the bimolecular quenching rate, and \(\tau_0\) the unquenched lifetime.



Demonstration of collisional quenching: quinine fluorescence (blue) is quenched by Cl\(^-\) ions — the glass on the right contains added NaCl, visibly reducing fluorescence intensity under UV excitation.





Mechanism of collisional quenching: the excited fluorophore undergoes bimolecular energy transfer to the quencher upon collision, returning to the ground state without photon emission.

title: 3  Fluorophores and Labeling Strategies
text:
3.5 Targeting Fluorescence to Specific Structures
Most biological structures are not intrinsically fluorescent — specific labeling strategies are required. Two broad approaches exist: non-covalent targeting (exploiting specific affinity) and covalent conjugation (chemical attachment of a fluorophore to a biomolecule).

3.5.1 Intercalating and Affinity Dyes
Some fluorophores report on specific molecular environments through non-covalent interactions. Ethidium bromide (EtBr) intercalates between DNA base pairs via \(\pi\)-stacking, with a fluorescence enhancement of several orders of magnitude upon intercalation — making it a classic DNA stain in gel electrophoresis and imaging. DPH (1,6-diphenyl-1,3,5-hexatriene) inserts into lipid bilayers and is essentially non-fluorescent in water, providing intrinsic membrane selectivity.



Ethidium bromide (EtBr, top) and DPH (bottom) as examples of affinity-based fluorescent probes. EtBr intercalates in double-stranded DNA (Ex/Em: 518/605 nm); DPH partitions into lipid bilayers.




3.5.2 Immunolabeling with IgG
Antibody-based labeling is the most versatile approach for targeting fluorescence to specific proteins. An IgG antibody (MW \(\approx 150\,\mathrm{kDa}\), \(\sim 1500\) amino acids) consists of two heavy chains and two light chains; antigen recognition occurs at the variable Fab domains (region 1), while the Fc domain (region 2) is constant.



Schematic of an IgG antibody showing the two heavy chains (dark blue) and two light chains (green/light blue), with the antigen-binding Fab domains (1), the Fc domain (2), the hypervariable CDR loops (3), the Fc region (4), the variable light chain domain (5), and the hinge region (6).


Two immunolabeling strategies are used in practice:
Direct labeling: a fluorescent primary antibody binds directly to the antigen (1 step, simpler, less signal amplification).
Indirect labeling: a non-fluorescent primary antibody with high specificity binds first; a fluorescent secondary antibody directed against the Fc of the primary is then added. This amplifies signal (multiple secondary antibodies per primary) and allows reuse of the same fluorescent secondary across many primaries from the same host species.



Summary of direct and indirect immunolabeling strategies, highlighting the use of antibodies from different host species to achieve orthogonal multi-target staining.

title: 3  Fluorophores and Labeling Strategies
text:
3.6 Covalent Conjugation Chemistry
To produce fluorescent antibodies or other labeled biomolecules, fluorophores must be covalently attached. Two amino acid side chains are the primary conjugation targets: lysine (K, primary amine \(-\)NH\(_2\)) and cysteine (C, thiol \(-\)SH).



The two main conjugation targets in proteins — lysine (amine) and cysteine (thiol) — and the corresponding reactive fluorophore chemistries: FITC (isothiocyanate, reacts with NH\(_2\) to form a thiourea bond) and BODIPY maleimide (reacts with SH to form a thioether bond).


The isothiocyanate reaction (FITC + \(-\)NH\(_2\) → thiourea) is efficient at pH 8–9 and widely used for antibody labeling. Maleimide chemistry (\(-\)SH + maleimide → thioether) is more selective since free cysteines are rarer than lysines in most proteins, and can be exploited for site-specific labeling if a unique cysteine is engineered in.

title: 3  Fluorophores and Labeling Strategies
text:
3.7 Labeling Stoichiometry and Self-Quenching
Increasing the number of fluorophores per protein does not linearly increase fluorescence — above a threshold, fluorophores on the same molecule undergo self-quenching via FRET between identical dyes (homo-FRET) or collisional quenching. For an IgG antibody, fluorescence is maximized at approximately 5–8 fluorophores per antibody depending on the dye.



Conjugate fluorescence as a function of fluorophore-to-protein ratio (mol:mol) for four fluorophore families on IgG antibodies. Fluorescence peaks around 5–8 equivalents, then decreases due to self-quenching. Oregon Green variants outperform FITC due to lower susceptibility to self-quenching.

title: 3  Fluorophores and Labeling Strategies
text:
3.8 FRET: Förster Resonance Energy Transfer
When two fluorophores are in close proximity (typically 1–10 nm) and the emission spectrum of the donor overlaps with the absorption spectrum of the acceptor, non-radiative energy transfer can occur. This is FRET (Förster Resonance Energy Transfer), first described by Perrin (1900) and quantified by Förster (1946).
The FRET efficiency \(E\) depends on the donor-acceptor distance \(r\):
\[E = \frac{1}{1 + (r/R_0)^6}\]
where \(R_0\) is the Förster radius (typically 2–7 nm), defined as the distance at which \(E = 50\%\).
The spectral overlap integral \(J\) between donor emission and acceptor absorption is the key molecular parameter:
\[J = \int F_D(\lambda)\, \varepsilon_A(\lambda)\, \lambda^4\, d\lambda\]



Spectral overlap between the donor emission (\(\lambda^{ex}_D\), \(\lambda^{em}_D\)) and acceptor absorption (\(\lambda^{ex}_A\), \(\lambda^{em}_A\)) spectra. The shaded overlap region (black) determines the Förster integral \(J\) and hence \(R_0\).


FRET is used as a “molecular ruler” — its \(r^{-6}\) distance dependence makes it exquisitely sensitive to nanometer-scale changes in molecular conformation or complex formation.

title: 3  Fluorophores and Labeling Strategies
text:
3.9 Photobleaching
Photobleaching is the irreversible photochemical destruction of a fluorophore upon prolonged illumination, typically via reaction of the excited triplet state with molecular oxygen to form reactive oxygen species that damage the chromophore. It is the primary limitation on the total number of photons that can be collected from a single fluorophore.
Photobleaching rates differ dramatically between fluorophore families — cyanines and BODIPY dyes are generally more photostable than fluorescein under typical imaging conditions.



Sequential fluorescence images of a multiply-stained cell (DAPI, blue; mitochondria, red; cytoskeleton, green) showing the differential photobleaching kinetics of the three fluorophores over six successive frames. The red channel bleaches fastest, the green channel most slowly.








WarningPhotobleaching in quantitative imaging



Photobleaching introduces a systematic bias in time-lapse and multi-acquisition experiments. Always acquire the most photosensitive channel first, minimize illumination intensity, and use antifade reagents (e.g., DABCO, Vectashield, or enzymatic oxygen-scavenging systems for live imaging).

title: 3  Fluorophores and Labeling Strategies
section: 3.10 GFP: An Exception to Non-Fluorescent Life
text:
3.10 GFP: An Exception to Non-Fluorescent Life
Green Fluorescent Protein (GFP), originally isolated from the jellyfish Aequorea victoria by Shimomura (1962), sequenced and cloned by Prasher (1992), and expressed heterologously by Chalfie (1994), revolutionized cell biology. The 2008 Nobel Prize in Chemistry was awarded to Shimomura, Chalfie, and Tsien. GFP is a 238 amino-acid \(\beta\)-barrel protein whose chromophore forms autocatalytically from the tripeptide Ser65-Tyr66-Gly67.



GFP structure (two orientations of the \(\beta\)-barrel, with the chromophore in cyan at center) and its absorption/emission spectra. The excitation maximum at ~395 nm (minor peak at ~475 nm) and emission at ~509 nm place it in a spectral window close to fluorescein.



3.10.1 Chromophore Formation
The GFP chromophore forms post-translationally by a spontaneous autocatalytic reaction: cyclization of the Ser65-Tyr66-Gly67 sequence, followed by oxidation by molecular O\(_2\) (rate-limiting, \(\tau \approx 1\) h at 37°C). No external cofactors or enzymes are required — only O\(_2\).



Mechanism of GFP chromophore maturation from the Ser65-Tyr66-Gly67 tripeptide: cyclization (\(-\)H\(_2\)O), oxidation by O\(_2\) (\(\tau \approx 1\) h), and deprotonation steps yield the mature conjugated chromophore. The \(\beta\)-barrel protects the chromophore from solvent quenching, accounting for the high quantum yield (\(\phi \approx 0.8\)). (Heim & Tsien, PNAS 1994)




3.10.2 The Fluorescent Protein Color Palette
Mutagenesis of the chromophore-forming tripeptide and surrounding residues has produced a full palette of fluorescent proteins spanning the visible spectrum. Key engineering axes include shifting emission wavelength, improving brightness, shortening maturation time, and improving photostability.



Spectra and photophysical properties of the main fluorescent protein variants across the visible spectrum (from cyan to far-red), organized by wavelength class. The table (Shaner et al., Nature Methods, 2005) lists extinction coefficients, quantum yields, brightness, photostability (\(t_{0.5}\) bleach), and maturation times.




3.10.3 GFP as a Reporter of Gene Expression
Genetically encoding GFP downstream of a promoter of interest makes transcriptional activity directly observable by fluorescence — the gene of interest is fused to the GFP sequence, so its expression product is directly fluorescent. This approach is broadly used for:

Tracking protein localization in real time
Measuring promoter activity
Following cell fate in development and immunology




Schematic of GFP as a reporter gene: the regulatory sequence (promoter) of the gene of interest drives expression of a GFP fusion protein, whose fluorescence intensity directly reports transcriptional activity.

title: 4  The Fluorescence Microscope in Practice
text:
4.1 Operating Principle of Epifluorescence
In epifluorescence microscopy, the same objective serves both to illuminate the specimen and to collect the emitted fluorescence. The excitation light and fluorescence travel in opposite directions along the same optical axis, separated by a dichroic mirror and optical filters.
The three key optical elements are:
The efficiency of this separation depends critically on the Stokes shift of the fluorophore — a large Stokes shift allows steeper filter cut-offs and lower background.
crumbs: 4  The Fluorescence Microscope in Practice

title: 4  The Fluorescence Microscope in Practice
text:
Schematic of an epifluorescence microscope: broadband light from the source passes through an excitation filter, reflects off a dichroic mirror down through the objective onto the specimen. Fluorescence emitted upward passes through the dichroic mirror and an emission filter before reaching the detector.




Excitation filter: bandpass filter that selects the excitation wavelength from the broadband source
Dichroic mirror: reflects the excitation wavelength toward the sample, transmits the (red-shifted) emission wavelength toward the detector
Emission filter (barrier filter): rejects any residual excitation light, transmits only the fluorescence




Principle of excitation/emission separation: the exciter filter selects UV/visible excitation light; the barrier filter transmits only the emitted fluorescence. The Stokes shift is what makes this separation physically possible.
crumbs: 4  The Fluorescence Microscope in Practice

title: 4  The Fluorescence Microscope in Practice
text:
4.2 Widefield vs. Laser Scanning
Two fundamental illumination geometries exist in fluorescence microscopy:



Comparison of widefield (left) and laser scanning (right) microscopy geometries. In widefield, the entire field is illuminated simultaneously and imaged onto a camera. In laser scanning, a focused spot is raster-scanned across the sample and detected point by point.


Widefield illumination is faster (full-frame acquisition) but collects out-of-focus fluorescence, degrading contrast in thick samples. Laser scanning trades speed for optical sectioning capability.
crumbs: 4  The Fluorescence Microscope in Practice

title: 4  The Fluorescence Microscope in Practice
text:
4.3 Light Sources

4.3.1 Mercury and Xenon Arc Lamps
The traditional light source for widefield epifluorescence is a high-pressure mercury (HBO) or xenon arc lamp. Mercury lamps have strong emission lines at 365, 405, 436, 546, and 579 nm — these happen to match common fluorophore excitation bands reasonably well, but are not tunable. Xenon lamps provide a flatter, more continuous spectrum across the visible range, better for quantitative spectroscopy.


4.3.2 Monochromatic Sources: Lasers and LEDs
Modern systems increasingly use monochromatic sources:



Emission spectra of krypton (top) and argon (bottom) ion lasers, showing the discrete laser lines available for fluorescence excitation. Argon lasers provide strong lines at 488 nm (FITC/GFP) and 514 nm; krypton adds lines at 568 nm and 647 nm.


LEDs have largely replaced arc lamps in many applications — they offer fast electronic switching (no mechanical shutters needed), long lifetime, stable output, and narrow enough spectra to reduce the need for excitation filters.
crumbs: 4  The Fluorescence Microscope in Practice

title: 4  The Fluorescence Microscope in Practice
text:
4.4 Filter Cubes and Filter Sets
A filter cube (or filter block) integrates the excitation filter, dichroic mirror, and emission filter into a single interchangeable unit. Most microscopes have a turret carrying 4–6 cubes, allowing rapid switching between fluorescence channels.



Fluorescence filter set configurations: (a) filter block turret with excitation interference filters, dichroic mirror, and emission filter; (b) individual filter components; (c) multiband excitation filter slider and multiband emission filter slider for simultaneous multi-color imaging.


Filter specifications are characterized by their center wavelength and bandwidth (e.g., “525/50” means centered at 525 nm with a 50 nm bandpass). Longpass emission filters (e.g., “LP515”) transmit all wavelengths above the cut-on wavelength.



Transmission spectra of three longpass emission filters (Semrock), showing the steep cut-on edges achievable with modern interference filter technology. The sharp transitions (\(<5\) nm) are critical for rejecting excitation light while passing fluorescence with minimal loss.








NoteWhy so many filters?



Each fluorophore requires a matched set of excitation filter, dichroic, and emission filter optimized for its specific excitation and emission peaks. Multicolor experiments require either multiple cubes (sequential acquisition) or multiband dichroics and emission filters (simultaneous acquisition, but with some spectral crosstalk trade-offs).

title: 5  Digital Imaging
text:
5.1 From Photons to Pixels: The Detection Chain
A fluorescence microscope is a photon counting instrument. Understanding the detection chain — from photon arrival at the sensor to the final pixel value — is essential for interpreting images quantitatively and designing experiments correctly.

title: 5  Digital Imaging
text:
5.2 CCD Cameras
A scientific CCD (charge-coupled device) camera consists of a photodiode array, thermoelectric cooling (Peltier), and readout electronics. Cooling reduces dark current (thermal electron generation), which is a major noise source in long exposures.



Cross-section of a cooled scientific CCD camera, showing the CCD sensor, thermoelectric cooler (TEC), vacuum seal, and precision readout electronics. Scientific CCDs are monochrome (no Bayer mosaic) to maximize quantum efficiency.





Micrograph of a CCD photodiode array integrated circuit, with an inset showing enlarged pixel elements with a Bayer color mosaic filter (used in consumer cameras but not in scientific monochrome CCDs).



5.2.1 Charge Transfer Principle
In a CCD, photoelectrons generated in each pixel are shifted along the sensor column by column to a single readout amplifier, using a sequence of clocked voltage gates on a p-Si substrate:



Schematic of CCD charge transfer: a positive voltage on one gate (red, +V) accumulates photoelectrons in the potential well beneath it. Sequential clocking of the gates shifts the charge packet laterally toward the readout amplifier.


The key consequence: all pixels share a single amplifier, so read noise is added only once per frame. This gives CCDs an excellent read noise floor (\(\sim 2\)–\(5\,e^-\) for scientific CCDs), critical for low-light fluorescence imaging.

title: 5  Digital Imaging
text:
5.3 sCMOS Cameras
Scientific CMOS (complementary metal-oxide-semiconductor) sensors have largely replaced CCDs in modern fluorescence microscopy. Each pixel has its own amplifier, enabling parallel readout of all pixels simultaneously:



Architecture of a CMOS sensor: each pixel contains a photodiode and an individual amplifier (photon-to-electron and electron-to-voltage conversion per pixel). Column amplifiers and analog-to-digital conversion deliver the digital image output.

title: 5  Digital Imaging
text:
5.4 CCD vs. sCMOS



Comparison of CCD (left) and CMOS (right) readout architectures. In CCD, charge is shifted sequentially through transfer registers to a single amplifier. In CMOS, each pixel has its own amplifier and is read independently via metal wire interconnects.


The key trade-offs:




CCD
sCMOS




Read noise
\(\sim 2\)–\(5\,e^-\)
\(\sim 1\)–\(2\,e^-\)


Frame rate
Moderate
Very high


Field of view
Moderate
Large


Dynamic range
High
High


Cost
Higher
Decreasing



sCMOS cameras now offer superior performance for most applications, with per-pixel noise maps allowing correction of pixel-to-pixel amplifier variation.

title: 5  Digital Imaging
section: 5.5 Digital Image: Bit Depth and Dynamic Range
text:
5.5 Digital Image: Bit Depth and Dynamic Range
The analog signal from the amplifier is digitized by an ADC (analog-to-digital converter). The bit depth determines how many gray levels are available:



Illustration of bit depth and gray levels: from 2-bit (4 gray levels) to 10-bit (1024 gray levels). Scientific cameras typically use 12–16 bits (4096–65536 gray levels), essential for quantitative fluorescence measurements.


Dynamic range is the ratio of the maximum signal (full-well capacity) to the minimum detectable signal (noise floor). For quantitative imaging, you need sufficient bit depth to sample this range without losing information at either end.

title: 5  Digital Imaging
text:
5.6 Noise Sources
The total noise in a fluorescence image has three main contributions:
Photon shot noise (\(\sigma_{\text{shot}} = \sqrt{N}\)): fundamental, follows Poisson statistics. Unavoidable — it arises from the quantum nature of light. Signal-to-noise ratio scales as \(\sqrt{N}\), so collecting more photons always helps.
Read noise (\(\sigma_{\text{read}}\)): added by the amplifier during readout. Fixed per pixel per frame — reduced by cooling and by sCMOS architecture.
Dark current (\(i_d\)): thermally generated electrons accumulated during exposure. Proportional to exposure time; dramatically reduced by Peltier cooling (\(\sim 2\times\) reduction per 7°C decrease in temperature).
The total noise in quadrature: \[\sigma_{\text{total}}^2 = N_{\text{signal}} + N_{\text{background}} + \sigma_{\text{read}}^2 + i_d \cdot t\]

title: 5  Digital Imaging
text:
5.7 Objectives and Numerical Aperture
The objective is the most critical optical element in the microscope. Its numerical aperture (NA) determines both light collection efficiency and lateral resolution:
\[\mathrm{NA} = n \sin\mu\]
where \(n\) is the refractive index of the immersion medium and \(\mu\) is the half-angle of the acceptance cone.



Effect of numerical aperture on light collection and Airy disc size: (a) \(\mu = 7°\), NA = 0.12; (b) \(\mu = 20°\), NA = 0.34; (c) \(\mu = 60°\), NA = 0.87. Higher NA collects more light and produces a smaller Airy disc.


Light collection scales as \(\mathrm{NA}^2\), so going from NA 0.5 to NA 1.4 gives nearly \(8\times\) more photons per pixel — a massive practical advantage.

title: 5  Digital Imaging
section: 5.8 Resolution: The Airy Disc and Rayleigh Criterion
text:
5.8 Resolution: The Airy Disc and Rayleigh Criterion
A point source imaged through a circular aperture produces an Airy diffraction pattern — a bright central disc surrounded by concentric rings. Two point sources are considered resolved (Rayleigh criterion) when the central maximum of one coincides with the first minimum of the other:



The Airy disc intensity distributions for two point sources: (a) clearly resolved, (b) at the Rayleigh limit, (c) unresolved. The 3D profiles show the intensity distributions and the corresponding top-view Airy patterns.


The lateral resolution (Rayleigh criterion) is:
\[R_{xy} = \frac{1.22\,\lambda}{2\,\mathrm{NA}} = \frac{0.61\,\lambda}{\mathrm{NA}}\]
For a typical oil-immersion objective (NA = 1.4) at \(\lambda = 500\,\mathrm{nm}\): \[R_{xy} \approx \frac{0.61 \times 500\,\mathrm{nm}}{1.4} \approx 218\,\mathrm{nm}\]
The Airy intensity pattern is formally: \[I(P) = I_{\max}\left[\frac{J_1(ka\sin\theta)}{ka\sin\theta}\right]^2\]
where \(J_1\) is the first-order Bessel function, \(k = 2\pi/\lambda\), and \(a\) is the aperture radius.

title: 5  Digital Imaging
text:
5.9 Reading an Objective
All key specifications are encoded on the objective barrel:



Annotated diagram of an Olympus Plan Apo 60×/1.40 Oil objective, showing the manufacturer, aberration correction class (Plan Apo), magnification (60×), numerical aperture (1.40), immersion medium (oil), tube length (\(\infty\)), cover slip thickness (0.17 mm), and working distance (0.21 mm).


Aberration correction classes (in increasing order of correction): - Achromat: corrected for chromatic aberration at 2 wavelengths - Plan: flat-field correction (no field curvature) - Apochromat (Apo): corrected at 3–4 wavelengths, highest chromatic correction - Plan Apo: both flat-field and full chromatic correction — the gold standard for fluorescence

title: 5  Digital Imaging
text:
5.10 Aberrations

5.10.1 Chromatic Aberration
Different wavelengths focus at different axial positions due to the wavelength dependence of the refractive index. This causes color fringing and z-offset between channels in multicolor imaging.



Axial chromatic aberration: a single lens focuses blue (450 nm), green (550 nm), and red (650 nm) light at different positions (blur \(\approx 0.30\) mm). An achromat doublet corrects this to a residual blur of \(\sim 0.01\) mm by combining positive and negative elements of different glass types.




5.10.2 Coma
Coma affects off-axis point sources, which appear comet-shaped due to the different contributions of lens zones at different radial distances from the optical axis.



Off-axis comatic aberration: rays from different lens zones (1–4) converge at different lateral positions, producing a characteristic comet-shaped blur for off-axis point sources.




5.10.3 Astigmatism
Astigmatism arises when the lens focuses rays in the sagittal and tangential planes at different axial positions. Between the two focal planes lies a “circle of least confusion.”



Astigmatism aberration: the sagittal and tangential focal planes are separated axially, producing characteristic elongated Airy patterns that rotate 90° between the two foci. The circle of least confusion lies between them.




5.10.4 Field Curvature
A flat specimen is imaged onto a curved (Petzval) surface, so the center and edges of the field cannot be simultaneously in focus with a simple lens. “Plan” objectives correct this.



Curvature of field: a flat object is imaged onto the curved Petzval surface by a simple lens. Flat-field (“Plan”) objectives introduce corrective elements to flatten this surface.








NotePractical implications for quantitative imaging



Chromatic aberration causes channel-dependent z-offsets — critical to correct for colocalization analyses. Field curvature means intensity is not uniform across the field — always flat-field correct before quantitative measurements. Both are dramatically reduced in Plan Apo objectives.

title: 6  Advanced Fluorescence Techniques
text:
6.1 Limits of Epifluorescence
Standard widefield epifluorescence illuminates the entire sample volume — including out-of-focus planes. In thick specimens (\(> 5\,\mu\)m), this generates strong background fluorescence that degrades contrast and signal-to-noise. Three strategies address this: confocal microscopy (optical sectioning by spatial filtering), TIRF microscopy (evanescent field illumination confined to \(\sim 100\) nm), and light-sheet microscopy (orthogonal illumination).

title: 6  Advanced Fluorescence Techniques
text:
6.2 Confocal Microscopy

6.2.1 Principle
Confocal microscopy achieves optical sectioning by placing a pinhole at the image plane conjugate to the focal point. Out-of-focus fluorescence, which would otherwise reach the detector in widefield, is geometrically rejected by this pinhole:



Confocal microscopy principle: (A) a laser focused to a point illuminates the specimen; in-focus emission (B) converges at the detector pinhole and passes to the PMT; out-of-focus emission (C) is defocused at the pinhole plane and largely rejected.





Light path in a confocal microscope: laser excitation passes through a source pinhole, reflects off a dichroic mirror, and is focused by the objective. In-focus fluorescence is re-collimated, passes through the dichroic, and focuses at the detector aperture (pinhole) before reaching the photomultiplier. Out-of-focus light is defocused at the pinhole and blocked.


The key consequences of confocal detection:

Optical sectioning: \(z\)-resolution \(\approx 500\)–\(800\) nm (vs. no \(z\)-selectivity in widefield)
Improved contrast in thick specimens
3D reconstruction by \(z\)-stack acquisition
Slower than widefield — point-by-point scanning requires \(\sim 1\)–\(30\) s per frame



6.2.2 FLIM: Fluorescence Lifetime Imaging Microscopy
Confocal systems can measure not only fluorescence intensity but also the fluorescence lifetime \(\tau\) at each pixel. Since \(\tau\) is sensitive to the local molecular environment (viscosity, pH, FRET, protein-protein interactions) independently of fluorophore concentration, FLIM provides information inaccessible from intensity images alone.



FLIM decay curves: (a) fluorescence lifetime measurements as a function of quencher concentration (Cl\(^-\)), showing the shortening of \(\tau\) with increasing [Q]. (b) Equivalent data showing the concentration-dependent decay. The lifetime is extracted by fitting the exponential decay \(I(t) = I_0 e^{-t/\tau}\) at each pixel.

title: 6  Advanced Fluorescence Techniques
text:
6.3 Evanescent Wave Microscopy (TIRF)

6.3.1 Snell-Descartes Law and the Critical Angle
When light travels from a dense medium (\(n_1\), e.g. glass, \(n = 1.518\)) to a less dense medium (\(n_2\), e.g. water, \(n = 1.33\)), beyond the critical angle \(\theta_c\) defined by:
\[\sin\theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}\]
total internal reflection (TIR) occurs. Rather than propagating into medium 2, the electromagnetic field decays exponentially as an evanescent wave with a characteristic penetration depth:
\[d = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi\sqrt{n_1^2\sin^2\theta - n_2^2}}\]
For typical parameters (\(\lambda = 488\) nm, \(\theta = 68°\)), \(d \approx 100\)–\(200\) nm — confining excitation to a thin layer at the glass-water interface.


6.3.2 TIRF in Practice: High-NA Objectives
Objective-based TIRF uses a high-NA oil-immersion objective (NA \(> 1.38\)) to launch the laser at angles beyond \(\theta_c\):



Objective-based TIRF: laser light entering the periphery of a high-NA objective (NA \(> 1.38\)) at angles exceeding the critical angle undergoes TIR at the coverslip/medium interface, generating an evanescent field. Light at lower angles (NA \(< 1.38\)) is transmitted into the sample.





TIRF principle: the evanescent wave excites only fluorophores within \(\sim 100\)–\(200\) nm of the coverslip surface (cell membrane, adhesion contacts), while fluorophores deeper in the cell are not excited.




6.3.3 Image Quality
The contrast improvement over widefield is dramatic:



Comparison of the same fluorescent bead sample imaged by TIRF (left) and widefield fluorescence (right). TIRF shows sharp, isolated single fluorophore spots against a dark background; widefield shows a bright, diffuse background that obscures individual molecules.




6.3.4 Application: Measuring Ligand-Receptor Binding Kinetics
TIRF is ideally suited to measure interactions at the membrane. The following example illustrates the use of TIRF to measure the binding kinetics between fluorescent liposomes carrying the glycosphingolipid Le\(^x\) and a supported lipid bilayer (SLB) presenting the cognate receptor:



TIRF-based \(K_d\) measurement: (a) fluorescent liposomes docking on an SLB under TIRF illumination; (b) lipid and fluorophore composition of the system; (c) TIRF image showing individual surface-bound liposomes (left) and a single-liposome intensity kymograph showing discrete on/off docking events used to extract residence times and binding rates.

title: 6  Advanced Fluorescence Techniques
text:
6.4 Super-Resolution Microscopy

6.4.1 Breaking the Diffraction Limit
The Rayleigh criterion (\(d \approx 200\)–\(300\) nm for visible light) is a fundamental limit for conventional microscopy. Super-resolution methods circumvent it not by improving optics, but by exploiting the photophysics of individual fluorophores to separate their emission temporally.


6.4.2 Localization-Based Super-Resolution (STORM/PALM)
The key idea: if only a sparse subset of fluorophores are emitting at any one time, each can be localized with precision far better than the diffraction limit by fitting a Gaussian to its point spread function:
\[\sigma_{\text{loc}} \approx \frac{\sigma_{\text{PSF}}}{\sqrt{N}}\]
where \(N\) is the number of detected photons. With \(N \approx 1000\) photons, localization precision of \(\sim 10\)–\(20\) nm is achievable.



dSTORM/PALM principle: (1) photoswitchable fluorophores stochastically switch between dark and bright states (\(k_{\text{on}}\), \(k_{\text{off}}\)); (2) each active emitter is localized by Gaussian fitting to its diffraction-limited spot; (3) temporal separation ensures that at each frame only a sparse, resolvable subset is active. After thousands of frames, the accumulated localizations build a super-resolved image.


The reconstruction process is analogous to determining positions from a blurred ensemble image:



Illustration of the reconstruction principle using the Eiffel Tower as an analogy: (left) original blurred image; (center) detected particle positions from individual frames; (right) reconstructed super-resolved image from accumulated localizations.




6.4.3 Example 1: Microtubules



Comparison of widefield (top) and dSTORM (bottom) images of immunolabeled microtubules, with intensity profiles across a single filament. The widefield FWHM is \(\sim 350\) nm (diffraction-limited); dSTORM resolves the hollow microtubule structure with a measured width below 50 nm.




6.4.4 Example 2: Nuclear Pore Proteins



dSTORM imaging of nuclear pore complexes (NPCs) in Xenopus oocyte nuclei: widefield (top-left) vs. dSTORM (bottom-left) reveal the 8-fold symmetry of individual NPCs (center, scale bar 200 nm). Two-color dSTORM (right) resolves the central pore (WGA-Alexa 488, green) from the periphery (gp210-Alexa 647, red).

title: 6  Advanced Fluorescence Techniques
text:
6.5 SPIM: Single Plane Illumination Microscopy
SPIM (also called light-sheet microscopy) illuminates the sample with a thin sheet of light oriented perpendicular to the detection axis, exciting fluorescence only in the focal plane of the objective.



SPIM geometry: the illumination axis and imaging axis are orthogonal. A thin light sheet confined to the \(xy\) plane illuminates only the focal plane being imaged, eliminating out-of-focus excitation entirely.


Key advantages: very low photobleaching and phototoxicity (only the imaged plane is illuminated), fast 3D imaging (no scanning needed), and suitability for large transparent specimens (embryos, organoids).

title: 6  Advanced Fluorescence Techniques
text:
6.6 FRAP: Fluorescence Recovery After Photobleaching
FRAP measures the lateral diffusion coefficient \(D\) of membrane or cytosolic components. A focused high-power laser pulse irreversibly bleaches fluorophores in a small region of interest. Fluorescence recovers as unbleached molecules from the surrounding area diffuse in.



FRAP on a supported lipid bilayer: (A) uniform fluorescence before bleaching; (B) a circular bleach spot is created; (C-D) fluorescence recovers as unbleached lipids diffuse laterally into the bleached region. The recovery kinetics yield \(D\) and the mobile fraction.


For a circular bleach spot of radius \(r\), the diffusion coefficient is:
\[D = \frac{r^2}{4 t_{1/2}}\]
where \(t_{1/2}\) is the half-time of fluorescence recovery.



FRAP applied to a cell: the bleached region (dark) recovers fluorescence as labeled molecules diffuse from the surrounding unbleached area. The mobile fraction is determined from the plateau recovery level relative to the pre-bleach intensity.

title: 6  Advanced Fluorescence Techniques
text:
6.7 FRET in Practice
Recall from the fluorophores lecture that FRET efficiency depends on \(r^{-6}\). In a biological context, FRET serves as a nanometer-scale proximity sensor: FRET occurs only when donor and acceptor are within \(\sim R_0 \approx 5\)–\(10\) nm.
The complete FRET framework — spectral overlap, distance dependence, and dipole orientation:



FRET in practice: (A) spectral overlap between donor emission and acceptor excitation spectra (shaded area = overlap integral \(J\)); (B) FRET efficiency \(E = R^{-6}/(R^{-6} + R_0^{-6})\) as a function of distance, with \(E = 0.5\) at \(r = R_0\); (C) dipole orientation dependence — FRET is maximal when the transition dipoles are parallel and zero when perpendicular.



6.7.1 Biological Application: Mechanosensing with FRET
FRET biosensors are increasingly used to report on intracellular forces. In the example below, an integrin-based tension sensor uses a TFP-Venus FRET pair connected by an elastic linker. When force is applied, the linker extends, increasing the TFP-Venus distance and reducing FRET efficiency:



Integrin tension sensor: (A) \(\alpha_v\beta_3\) integrins in adhesion complexes under tension; (B) the sensor construct — TFP and Venus flanking an elastic linker; FRET decreases when force extends the linker; (C) TFP, Venus, and FRET channel images of a cell, with the FRET index map (right) revealing spatial distribution of integrin-transmitted forces.

title: 7  RICM: Reflection Interference Contrast Microscopy
text:
7.1 Principle
RICM (Reflection Interference Contrast Microscopy) is a label-free technique that images the distance \(h(x)\) between a particle or cell membrane and a flat glass substrate, with nanometer axial sensitivity. It exploits the interference between light reflected at the glass-water interface (beam \(I_0\)) and light reflected at the bottom surface of the object (beam \(I_1\), \(I_2\)):
The reflected intensity varies as:
\[I(x) \propto I_0 + I_1 - 2\sqrt{I_0 I_1}\cos\left(\frac{4\pi n\, h(x)}{\lambda} + \phi_0\right)\]
where \(n\) is the refractive index of the medium, \(\lambda\) the illumination wavelength, and \(\phi_0\) an offset phase that depends on the Fresnel reflection coefficients. Bright fringes correspond to constructive interference, dark fringes to destructive interference.
crumbs: 7  RICM: Reflection Interference Contrast Microscopy

title: 7  RICM: Reflection Interference Contrast Microscopy
text: RICM geometry: light reflected at the glass surface (\(I_0\)) and at the bottom of the particle (\(I_1\), \(I_2\)) interfere, producing an intensity pattern that encodes the local gap \(h(x)\) between the particle and the substrate. The image is formed by the objective below.
crumbs: 7  RICM: Reflection Interference Contrast Microscopy

title: 7  RICM: Reflection Interference Contrast Microscopy
text:
7.2 Newton’s Rings: The Ideal Case
For a perfect sphere of radius \(R\) resting at height \(h_0\) above the substrate, \(h(x) = h_0 + x^2/2R\), giving concentric circular fringes — Newton’s rings:



Ideal Newton’s rings pattern from a sphere close to a flat surface: the fringe spacing encodes the local curvature and hence the sphere radius \(R\); the central dark spot indicates the region of minimum separation.
crumbs: 7  RICM: Reflection Interference Contrast Microscopy

title: 7  RICM: Reflection Interference Contrast Microscopy
text:
7.3 Reading the Fringes
The fringe pattern maps \(h(x)\) through the phase of the cosine. Between two consecutive fringes (dark-to-dark or bright-to-bright), \(h\) increases by \(\lambda/2n \approx 180\)–\(200\) nm for green light in water. Lateral resolution is limited by the Rayleigh criterion (\(\sim 200\)–\(300\) nm), setting the minimum resolvable \(\Delta x\).



Fringe reading: each fringe order \(N\) corresponds to a specific \(h\) value, allowing \(h(x)\) to be read off from the fringe positions. The inset shows the local geometry for extracting the height between two consecutive fringe positions \(x_{N-1}\) and \(x_N\).
crumbs: 7  RICM: Reflection Interference Contrast Microscopy

title: 7  RICM: Reflection Interference Contrast Microscopy
text:
7.4 Microscope Setup
RICM uses reflected-light illumination with a monochromatic source (interference filter on an HBO lamp) and a standard reflected-light microscope with an antiflex objective (with crossed polarizers to suppress specular reflections from the lens surfaces):



RICM microscope setup: monochromatic light from an HBO lamp passes through a field diaphragm and interference filter, reflects off a beam splitter toward the objective, illuminates the sample (particle on a coverslip), and the reflected light is collected by the same objective and directed to the CCD camera.
crumbs: 7  RICM: Reflection Interference Contrast Microscopy

title: 7  RICM: Reflection Interference Contrast Microscopy
section: 7.5 Glass Bead on Glass: A Reference Experiment
text:
7.5 Glass Bead on Glass: A Reference Experiment
A glass bead (\(n \approx 1.52\)) sedimenting on a glass coverslip in water generates a clean Newton’s ring pattern that can be quantitatively modeled:



(a) Cross-section geometry of a glass bead of radius \(R\) at height \(h\) above the substrate. (b) Experimental RICM image showing Newton’s rings from the glass bead, with clearly resolved fringes (scale bar 10 \(\mu\)m).
crumbs: 7  RICM: Reflection Interference Contrast Microscopy

title: 7  RICM: Reflection Interference Contrast Microscopy
text:
7.6 Effect of Numerical Aperture
A key experimental limitation of RICM is that increasing NA increases the range of illumination angles, which averages out the fringe contrast. Higher-order fringes (large \(h\)) are more sensitive to this averaging than the central fringes. This sets a practical upper limit on the measurable \(h\) — typically \(\sim 500\)–\(1000\) nm for standard antiflex objectives.
crumbs: 7  RICM: Reflection Interference Contrast Microscopy

title: 7  RICM: Reflection Interference Contrast Microscopy
text:
7.7 Wetting and Contact Angles
RICM can directly image the contact line and contact angle of droplets or vesicles on surfaces, giving access to solid-liquid-air surface energies:



RICM image of a sessile droplet showing the contact angle \(\theta_C\) and the solid-liquid interfacial tension \(\gamma_{SL}\) at the contact line. The fringe pattern in the thin film region near the contact line directly encodes the film profile.
crumbs: 7  RICM: Reflection Interference Contrast Microscopy

title: 7  RICM: Reflection Interference Contrast Microscopy
section: 7.8 Absolute Height Measurement: The Dual Wavelength Solution
text:
7.8 Absolute Height Measurement: The Dual Wavelength Solution
Single-wavelength RICM measures \(h\) modulo \(\lambda/2n\) — i.e., fringe order ambiguity prevents absolute height determination beyond the first fringe. The dual-wavelength solution uses two illumination wavelengths \(\lambda_\alpha\) and \(\lambda_\beta\) simultaneously; the beat period \(\Lambda = \lambda_\alpha\lambda_\beta / |2n(\lambda_\alpha - \lambda_\beta)|\) is much larger, extending the unambiguous range to several micrometers.



Dual-wavelength RICM setup: an HBO lamp illuminates the sample simultaneously at \(\lambda_\alpha\) and \(\lambda_\beta\) (selected by an interference filter); the two wavelengths are separated by a dichroic mirror and detected on two synchronized CCD cameras. The height map \(h(x)\) is extracted from the two phase maps.
crumbs: 7  RICM: Reflection Interference Contrast Microscopy

title: 7  RICM: Reflection Interference Contrast Microscopy
section: 7.9 Colloidal Interactions: Measuring the Interaction Potential
text:
7.9 Colloidal Interactions: Measuring the Interaction Potential
For a colloidal particle near a surface, the equilibrium height distribution \(P(h)\) is related to the interaction potential \(V(h)\) via Boltzmann:
\[P(h) \propto \exp\left(-\frac{V(h)}{k_BT}\right)\]
RICM measures the instantaneous \(h(x,t)\) from the fringe pattern; recording many frames gives \(P(h)\) and hence \(V(h)\) with \(k_BT\) resolution.



Particle-surface interaction geometry: a charged colloidal particle (\(-\)) near a charged glass surface (\(-\)) in water experiences electrostatic repulsion, van der Waals attraction, and gravity. The mean height \(\langle h \rangle\) is set by the balance of these forces.





Radial intensity profile of the RICM fringe pattern from a single colloidal particle (data points) and theoretical fit (line). The fit yields the particle radius, height, and the amplitude of the interaction potential.
crumbs: 7  RICM: Reflection Interference Contrast Microscopy

title: 7  RICM: Reflection Interference Contrast Microscopy
text:
7.10 Cell Adhesion
RICM was originally developed to study cell adhesion. It directly images the contact zone between a cell and the substrate, without fluorescent labels. Close contact regions appear dark (small \(h\), destructive interference for appropriate parameters); non-adherent regions appear bright.

7.10.1 Modeling Cell Adhesion
The classic Bell-Evans-Dembo model describes cell adhesion as a competition between specific receptor-ligand bonds in the contact zone and repulsive interactions (glycocalyx, membrane tension) at the periphery:



Bell-Evans-Dembo model of cell-cell adhesion: in the contact region, specific cell-cell bonds (filled circles) bridge the two membranes; free receptors and glycocalyx molecules are excluded laterally. The separation distance in the contact zone is set by the bond length.




7.10.2 Liposomes on Glass
Tense liposomes on glass provide a model system with well-defined mechanics. Their RICM image shows a flat central contact zone surrounded by fringes from the curved membrane:



RICM image of a tense liposome on glass (left) and corresponding radial intensity profile with fit (right). The flat central dark region indicates close contact; the fringe pattern gives the membrane profile and the contact angle, from which the adhesion energy can be extracted.




7.10.3 Ligand-Receptor Interactions
Functionalizing the substrate and the liposome/cell membrane with complementary ligand-receptor pairs allows direct quantification of specific adhesion energies:



Schematic of a liposome or cell bearing receptors (blue) adhering to a substrate functionalized with ligands (orange triangles). Specific bonds form in the contact zone, bending the membrane inward; RICM images the contact geometry.




7.10.4 Using Membrane Fluctuations
For weakly adhered membranes, thermal fluctuations modulate \(h(x,t)\). The amplitude of fluctuations \(\langle u^2\rangle^{1/2}\) is suppressed in the contact zone (bonds restrict membrane motion) and recovers outside it — providing a spatially resolved map of bond density:



RICM image of a weakly adhered liposome (left) and the fluctuation amplitude profile \(\langle u^2\rangle^{1/2}\) along a transect (right). Fluctuations are suppressed in the contact zone where bonds are present and enhanced at the free membrane periphery.








NoteRICM in practice



RICM requires no fluorescent labels and gives nanometer-scale axial information in real time. Its main limitations are: (1) fringe order ambiguity (resolved by dual-wavelength or simulation); (2) sensitivity to refractive index inhomogeneities in the sample; (3) restricted to objects close to the substrate (\(h < 1\)–\(2\,\mu\)m for reliable fringe counting). It is most powerful when combined with fluorescence for multi-modal imaging.








TipResources for this chapter

Tools

Exercises

Given a RICM image of a glass bead (\(R = 5\,\mu\)m) with fringe spacing measured from the image: calculate the bead-substrate gap \(h_0\) and compare with the theoretical prediction from DLVO theory for a charged surface in 10 mM NaCl.

title: 8  Two-Photon Microscopy
text:
8.1 Principle: Nonlinear Excitation
Two-photon excitation (2PE) is a nonlinear optical process in which a fluorophore absorbs two photons simultaneously, each carrying half the energy required for the \(S_0 \to S_1\) transition. The probability of two-photon absorption scales as the square of the instantaneous intensity:
\[F_{2PE} \propto \sigma_2 \cdot I^2(t)\]
where \(\sigma_2\) is the two-photon absorption cross-section (measured in Göppert-Mayer units, \(1\,\mathrm{GM} = 10^{-50}\,\mathrm{cm^4\,s\,photon^{-1}}\)) and \(I\) is the instantaneous photon flux. This quadratic dependence is the fundamental origin of the technique’s most important properties.
To achieve measurable two-photon absorption rates without damaging the sample, it is essential to concentrate the excitation in both space (tight focusing) and time (ultrashort pulses).
A mode-locked Ti:sapphire laser delivers pulses of $$ 100–150 fs duration at ~80 MHz repetition rate — concentrating photons into brief bursts while maintaining a modest average power (\(<100\) mW at the sample):

title: 8  Two-Photon Microscopy
text: Pulsed excitation strategy for two-photon microscopy: a CW laser (top) distributes photons uniformly in time, giving a low instantaneous flux. A pulsed laser (bottom) compresses the same average power into very short pulses of duration \(\tau\), dramatically increasing the peak intensity and hence the two-photon excitation probability, while keeping average power (and therefore thermal load) low.

title: 8  Two-Photon Microscopy
text:
8.2 Intrinsic Optical Sectioning
Because 2PE scales as \(I^2\), excitation is confined to the focal volume where the intensity is highest. Out-of-focus planes contribute negligibly — even though light passes through them. This gives intrinsic optical sectioning without a pinhole.
The consequence is dramatically visible: in a cuvette of fluorescent dye, one-photon excitation (visible laser) illuminates the entire beam path, while two-photon excitation (near-IR, \(\sim 900\) nm) produces fluorescence only at the focus:



One-photon vs. two-photon excitation in a fluorescent dye cuvette under a 4× objective. Left: visible laser excitation — the entire beam path fluoresces. Right: near-IR pulsed excitation at the same objective — fluorescence is confined to the focal point, demonstrating intrinsic 3D confinement from the \(I^2\) dependence.

title: 8  Two-Photon Microscopy
text:
8.3 Why Near-Infrared?
Two-photon excitation uses wavelengths roughly twice those used for one-photon excitation — typically 700–1000 nm (near-IR). This has three major practical consequences:
Reduced scattering: biological tissue scatters light with a wavelength dependence of approximately \(\lambda^{-4}\) (Rayleigh) to \(\lambda^{-1}\) (Mie). Near-IR light penetrates far deeper than visible light — enabling imaging hundreds of microns into tissue, compared to $<$50 µm for confocal.
Reduced phototoxicity: near-IR photons are not absorbed by most endogenous chromophores (hemoglobin, flavins, NADH), reducing out-of-focus photodamage.
No pinhole needed: all collected fluorescence comes from the focal volume, so non-descanned detection with large-area photomultipliers maximizes signal collection even after scattering.

title: 8  Two-Photon Microscopy
text:
8.4 Imaging Deep into Tissue
The combination of near-IR excitation and intrinsic sectioning makes two-photon microscopy the method of choice for imaging intact tissue, in vivo preparations, and thick specimens where confocal fails:



Two-photon fluorescence image of a neuronal axon labeled with a red fluorophore in a thick tissue preparation. The long-range axonal projection and fine axon terminals are resolved deep in the tissue — a preparation inaccessible to one-photon confocal microscopy due to scattering.





Three-color two-photon image of a dense cell population in a thick tissue section, showing simultaneous imaging of three spectrally distinct fluorescent labels (magenta, green, cyan) deep in a scattering specimen.

title: 8  Two-Photon Microscopy
text:
8.5 Instrument Configuration
Two-photon microscopy uses a laser-scanning architecture similar to confocal, but with key differences: no pinhole, non-descanned detection preferred, and a Ti:sapphire (or fiber) pulsed laser source. Fast mechanical shutters control laser exposure and protect live samples:



Shutter systems for live-cell imaging: (a) illumination source shutter (Uniblitz electronic); (b) large-aperture rotary shutter with drive arm, aperture, and anodized housing; (c) filter wheel shutter. Shutters are essential for minimizing photobleaching and phototoxicity in time-lapse experiments.


A complete laser-scanning confocal/two-photon system integrates the scan head, microscope body, laser sources, and acquisition computers into a controlled workstation:



Complete Nikon laser scanning confocal microscope configuration (PCM 2000 scan head on a Nikon E1000 body): argon ion and helium-neon laser sources, DXM 1200 digital camera, confocal scanning head, halogen illuminator, filter cubes, and dual monitors for specimen image and software control.

title: 8  Two-Photon Microscopy
section: 8.6 Two-Photon vs. Confocal: When to Choose
text:
8.6 Two-Photon vs. Confocal: When to Choose









Confocal
Two-photon




Excitation wavelength
Visible (405–647 nm)
Near-IR (700–1000 nm)


Depth penetration
\(< 100\,\mu\)m in tissue
\(> 500\,\mu\)m in tissue


Sectioning mechanism
Pinhole
\(I^2\) nonlinearity


Out-of-focus bleaching
Yes
Minimal


Phototoxicity
Moderate
Low (out-of-focus)


Signal at depth
Drops quickly
Much more robust


Cost
Lower
Higher (Ti:Sa laser)



Two-photon excitation is the method of choice whenever depth penetration, reduced phototoxicity, or intrinsic third-harmonic generation (THG) for label-free structural imaging are needed. Confocal is generally preferable for thin specimens and multicolor experiments where near-IR two-photon cross-sections are limiting.






NoteRelation to SHG and THG imaging



Two-photon setups naturally enable second harmonic generation (SHG) and third harmonic generation (THG) imaging of non-centrosymmetric structures (collagen fibers, myosin filaments, lipid droplet interfaces) — entirely label-free. These coherent nonlinear signals appear at exactly \(\lambda/2\) or \(\lambda/3\) and are spectrally separable from two-photon fluorescence.
