Estimate the two-photon excitation rate for a fluorescein molecule (\(\sigma_2 \approx 38\,\mathrm{GM}\) at 800 nm) at the focus of a 100× / 1.4 NA objective with 10 mW average power and 100 fs pulses at 80 MHz. Compare with the one-photon rate under CW illumination at the same average power.

title: 9  Single-Molecule Fluorescence with Mercury Lamp Illumination
text:
9.1 Why Single-Molecule Imaging Matters
This exercise sheet accompanies the article by Unger et al. (1999), which demonstrated that single-molecule fluorescence can be observed with a standard mercury lamp and a cooled CCD camera. The problems are scaffolded step-by-step and cover: dilution and spatial separation, the Rayleigh resolution criterion, Poisson intuition for overlap, photon energy and photon flux, signal-to-noise, and photophysical signatures (blinking and bleaching).
crumbs: 9  Single-Molecule Fluorescence with Mercury Lamp Illumination

title: 9  Single-Molecule Fluorescence with Mercury Lamp Illumination
text:
Concept: Give one reason why measuring at the single-molecule level can reveal information hidden in bulk (ensemble) measurements. Provide a biological example.







TipCorrection





Bulk averaging can hide heterogeneity such as multiple activity states, conformational subpopulations, or time-dependent (dynamic) disorder. Examples: (i) Different turnover rates of individual enzyme molecules (Lu, Xie 1998). (ii) Stepping of single motor proteins (kinesin/myosin) that is smeared out in ensemble assays.




Accessibility: Why is the use of a mercury lamp and a standard CCD (instead of a laser confocal or TIRF with EMCCD) important for democratizing single-molecule studies?







TipCorrection





Because it shows that single-molecule sensitivity is attainable with widely available, lower-cost equipment. This reduces the barrier to entry for teaching labs and small groups.
crumbs: 9  Single-Molecule Fluorescence with Mercury Lamp Illumination

title: 9  Single-Molecule Fluorescence with Mercury Lamp Illumination
text:
9.2 Dilution and Molecular Separation
In the article, single dye molecules were deposited from very dilute solutions onto cleaned coverslips. Extreme dilution ensures spatial separations large enough to resolve and count molecules individually.

From molarity to surface density. Consider \(1\,\mu\mathrm{L}\) of a \(10^{-6}\,\mathrm{M}\) solution spread uniformly over \(1\,\mathrm{cm}^2\).

How many molecules are in \(1\,\mu\mathrm{L}\) at \(10^{-6}\,\mathrm{M}\)? (Use \(N_A=6.02\times10^{23}\,\mathrm{mol}^{-1}\).)
What area is available per molecule on the surface (in \(\mathrm{m}^2\))?
Estimate the mean intermolecular spacing by taking the square root of that area (convert to nm).








TipCorrection






\(n = C V = 10^{-6}\,\mathrm{mol/L}\times 10^{-6}\,\mathrm{L}=10^{-12}\,\mathrm{mol}\). Molecules: \(N=nN_A\approx 6.02\times10^{11}\).
Total area \(A=1\,\mathrm{cm}^2=1\times10^{-4}\,\mathrm{m}^2\). Area per molecule: \(A/N \approx 1.66\times10^{-16}\,\mathrm{m}^2\).
Mean spacing \(\ell \approx \sqrt{A/N}\approx \sqrt{1.66\times10^{-16}}\approx 1.29\times10^{-8}\,\mathrm{m}=12.9\,\mathrm{nm}\).





Now much more dilute: Repeat the calculation for a \(10^{-12}\,\mathrm{M}\) solution (still \(1\,\mu\mathrm{L}\) spread over \(1\,\mathrm{cm}^2\)).







TipCorrection





At \(10^{-12}\,\mathrm{M}\), \(n=10^{-18}\,\mathrm{mol}\) and \(N\approx 6.02\times10^{5}\) molecules. Area per molecule \(\approx 1.66\times10^{-10}\,\mathrm{m}^2\); spacing \(\ell\approx 12.9\,\mu\mathrm{m}\).
crumbs: 9  Single-Molecule Fluorescence with Mercury Lamp Illumination

title: 9  Single-Molecule Fluorescence with Mercury Lamp Illumination
text:
9.3 Resolution Limit: Rayleigh Criterion
The Rayleigh criterion estimates the smallest resolvable distance between two point sources:
\[d \approx \frac{0.61\,\lambda}{NA}\]

Take \(\lambda=550\,\mathrm{nm}\) and \(NA=1.4\). Compute \(d\).
Compare \(d\) to the mean separations found above. Explain why dilution is essential.







TipCorrection





\(d \approx 0.61\times 550\,\mathrm{nm}/1.4 \approx 240\,\mathrm{nm}\) (within the typical 200–300 nm diffraction limit).
At \(10^{-6}\,\mathrm{M}\), spacing (\(\sim13\,\mathrm{nm}\)) \(\ll d\): unresolved. At \(10^{-12}\,\mathrm{M}\), spacing (\(\sim13\,\mu\mathrm{m}\)) \(\gg d\): well-resolved single emitters.
crumbs: 9  Single-Molecule Fluorescence with Mercury Lamp Illumination

title: 9  Single-Molecule Fluorescence with Mercury Lamp Illumination
text:
9.4 Overlap Probability: Poisson Intuition
Assume each molecule appears as a \(\sim 0.5\,\mu\mathrm{m}\)-diameter fluorescence spot.

Using the mean spacing \(\sim 13\,\mu\mathrm{m}\) at \(10^{-12}\,\mathrm{M}\), argue qualitatively that the fraction of overlapping spots is very small (order \(\sim 1\%\)).
Why does reducing concentration further decrease the chance of overlap?







TipCorrection





The spot diameter is \(\sim 0.5\,\mu\)m, whereas mean spacing is \(\sim 13\,\mu\)m; the area ratio \((0.5/13)^2 \approx 0.0015 \approx 0.15\%\) suggests a very small overlap probability. Lower concentration increases spacing as \(\propto C^{-1/2}\) (on a surface), reducing overlap probability rapidly.
crumbs: 9  Single-Molecule Fluorescence with Mercury Lamp Illumination

title: 9  Single-Molecule Fluorescence with Mercury Lamp Illumination
text:
9.5 Photon Energy and Photon Flux

Energy per photon. Use \(E=\dfrac{hc}{\lambda}\) with \(h=6.63\times10^{-34}\,\mathrm{J\cdot s}\), \(c=3.00\times10^8\,\mathrm{m/s}\), and \(\lambda=550\,\mathrm{nm}\) to compute the photon energy in joules and in electronvolts (\(1\,\mathrm{eV}=1.6\times10^{-19}\,\mathrm{J}\)).







TipCorrection





\[E=\frac{6.63\times10^{-34}\times 3.00\times10^8}{550\times10^{-9}}\approx 3.61\times10^{-19}\,\mathrm{J} \approx 2.26\,\mathrm{eV}\]




Photon flux from irradiance. The mercury lamp gives \(\approx 4.8\times 10^{5}\,\mathrm{W/m^2}\) at the sample. Convert this irradiance into photons\(\cdot\mathrm{m}^{-2}\cdot\mathrm{s}^{-1}\).







TipCorrection





\[\Phi = \frac{I}{E} \approx \frac{4.8\times10^{5}}{3.61\times10^{-19}}\approx 1.33\times10^{24}\ \mathrm{photons\,m^{-2}\,s^{-1}}\]




Absorptions per second (single molecule). A rhodamine molecule has absorption cross-section \(\sigma_a \approx 4.0\times10^{-20}\,\mathrm{m^2}\). Estimate its absorbed photons per second.







TipCorrection





\[\text{Absorptions} \approx \Phi\,\sigma_a \approx 1.33\times10^{24}\times 4.0\times10^{-20}\approx 5.3\times10^{4}\ \mathrm{s^{-1}}\]




Emitted photons and detected electrons. With quantum yield \(\varphi\approx 0.9\), objective collection \(\approx 31\%\), and CCD quantum efficiency \(\approx 40\%\), estimate detected photoelectrons per second.







TipCorrection





Emitted: \(0.9\times 5.3\times10^{4}\approx 4.8\times10^{4}\ \mathrm{s^{-1}}\)
Collected by objective: \(0.31\times 4.8\times10^{4}\approx 1.49\times10^{4}\ \mathrm{s^{-1}}\)
Detected (QE 40%): \(0.4\times 1.49\times10^{4}\approx 6.0\times10^{3}\ \mathrm{e^{-}\,s^{-1}}\) per molecule.
crumbs: 9  Single-Molecule Fluorescence with Mercury Lamp Illumination

title: 9  Single-Molecule Fluorescence with Mercury Lamp Illumination
text:
9.6 Signal-to-Noise

Explain why both integrated (“astronomy”) S/N and peak-pixel S/N are useful for evaluating single-molecule detectability on CCD images.
If background is \(\sim 28\) counts with RMS \(\sim 5.9\) counts per pixel, and a molecule yields \(\sim 2500\) counts/s (integrated), explain qualitatively why S/N is sufficient for detection in \(\sim 1\) s exposures.







TipCorrection





Peak-pixel S/N reflects the visibility of the brightest pixel(s), important for spot finding; integrated S/N reflects the total certainty over a chosen box. Given \(\sim 2500\) counts/s signal vs background RMS \(\sim 6\) counts/pixel and modest integration over \(\sim 25\) pixels, the signal substantially exceeds noise, enabling reliable detection in \(\sim 1\) s.
crumbs: 9  Single-Molecule Fluorescence with Mercury Lamp Illumination

title: 9  Single-Molecule Fluorescence with Mercury Lamp Illumination
text:
9.7 Blinking and Photobleaching

Define photobleaching and explain why stepwise (quantized) bleaching supports the single-molecule interpretation.
What does fluorescence blinking (on/off flickering) suggest about the photophysics of individual emitters?







TipCorrection





Bleaching: irreversible loss of fluorescence due to photochemical damage or transition to a permanent dark state. Single emitters bleach in a single discrete step, unlike the gradual decay observed from ensembles — this is a key signature of single-molecule detection.
Blinking: stochastic transitions between emissive and non-emissive states (e.g., triplet or radical states), another hallmark of single-molecule behavior invisible in ensemble measurements.




Primary Reference:
Unger, M. A.; Kartalov, E.; Chiu, C.-S.; Lester, H. A.; & Quake, S. R. (1999). Single-molecule fluorescence observed with mercury lamp illumination. BioTechniques, 27(5), 1008–1014. DOI: 10.2144/99275rr01.
